name: Morag
description: Morag is the name given to a cryptid said to inhabit Loch Morar in Scotland. Similar to the Loch Ness Monster, Morag is described as a large, serpentine creature, often with rough skin, humps, and a dark coloration. Sightings date back centuries, with increased attention following the publicity surrounding Nessie. Proponents suggest Morag is a surviving plesiosaur, a relic population of an unknown aquatic creature, or a misidentified large fish. Evidence cited includes eyewitness accounts, sonar readings interpreted as large moving objects, and anecdotal evidence of disturbances on the loch's surface. Mainstream science dismisses Morag as a misidentification of known animals (such as otters or large fish), wave action, floating debris, or hoaxes. The depth and size of Loch Morar make definitive scientific investigation challenging, and the lack of photographic or video evidence considered conclusive contributes to the skepticism. Unlike Loch Ness, Loch Morar has received far less intensive scientific investigation, making concrete evidence even more scarce. The legend of Morag persists in local folklore and continues to attract the interest of cryptozoologists and monster hunters.
subtitle: Loch Morar's elusive lake monster.

claims.claim: Morag is a large, unknown aquatic creature.
claims.evidence: Eyewitness accounts describing a serpentine creature.
claims.criticism: Eyewitness accounts are subjective and prone to misidentification or embellishment.

claims.claim: Sonar readings show evidence of large, moving objects in Loch Morar.
claims.evidence: Unexplained sonar anomalies detected in the loch.
claims.criticism: Sonar anomalies can be caused by various factors, including equipment malfunction, geological formations, or known aquatic life.

claims.evidence: Comparisons between Morag descriptions and plesiosaur anatomy.
claims.criticism: There is no fossil evidence to support the survival of plesiosaurs beyond the Cretaceous period, and the loch environment is unlikely to support such a creature.

keyFigures.name: Adrian Shine
keyFigures.role: Loch Ness Project leader, skeptical of Nessie/Morag claims

timeline.event: Earliest possible mentions of lake monsters in Scotland.
Reported sighting of a strange creature in Loch Morar.
Alleged sighting of Morag by Duncan McDonnell and William Simpson, who claimed the creature attacked their boat.

sources.author: Bauer, Henry H.
sources.work: The Enigma of Loch Ness: Science Looks at the Evidence
sources.title: Bauer, Henry H.
